turns.text: Hello Deidre, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels… strange to be on this side of the microphone. Usually, I’m the one typing out articles, explaining the mechanics of progesterone or the timeline of organogenesis to my readers. It’s funny—I’ve built this whole career around making pregnancy sound like a beautiful, predictable clockwork process. But sitting here now, that knowledge feels like a heavy coat I can’t quite take off.

I’m currently navigating the space between wanting this so badly and being absolutely terrified of the "what ifs." My last experience didn’t end the way I’d written it would in my head. That silence in the ultrasound room—it’s the loudest thing I’ve ever heard. It’s hard to reconcile the biology I know with the reality of what happened to me. 

Everything about this process feels like a hurdle right now. Being "medium risk" just means I have more appointments, more commuting on the Red Line when I’m already feeling wiped out, and more time sitting in sterile rooms alone while my husband is at work. I keep playing the "informed patient" to keep from falling apart, but honestly? I just want someone to look at me and see the person, not the clinical risk profile. Does that make sense?

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Honestly? I expected a partnership. I think I had this naive vision of walking into an office, sitting down with a provider who would look at my chart—and my history—and say, "Okay, Deidre, we know this is heavy, let’s build a map together." I wanted someone to acknowledge that my background in embryology isn’t just a list of facts, but a layer of armor I’ve put on to protect myself from the grief I still carry.

The reality has been… clinical. It’s efficient, sure, but it’s so transactional. When I show up for these visits, I’m usually exhausted from the trek—switching trains, dodging tourists, trying to time my nausea so I don’t lose it on the platform. By the time I get to the exam room, I’m just another appointment block. My providers see the "medium risk" label and start talking about blood pressure and screenings, and I find myself slipping into "researcher mode" just to keep up. I start throwing around terms like "luteal phase" or "hCG doubling times" because that’s the only way they seem to engage with me—as a data point rather than a woman who’s holding her breath. I wanted support, but I think what I’m actually getting is just medical management. It leaves me feeling incredibly lonely.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I expected that because I’ve spent years "studying" the mechanics of life, I would be shielded by that expertise. I thought I’d be able to approach this with this cool, detached sense of preparedness. I pictured myself being the woman who knew exactly which tests to ask for, someone who could handle the volatility because I knew the "why" behind every biological process. I genuinely believed that knowledge would be my superpower.

But the reality is that the science hasn’t protected me at all; it’s actually made the waiting harder. Instead of just feeling "pregnant," I find myself obsessively tracking every minute detail, turning my body into a laboratory. Every time I feel a cramp, I’m not just feeling a sensation—I’m mentally dissecting it, mapping it to a gestational week, and waiting for the other shoe to drop. 

It’s exhausting to be your own doctor and your own patient at the same time. I’m not "ready" in the way I thought I’d be. I’m hyper-vigilant, constantly waiting for the silence to return. I thought I’d be walking toward a dream, but most days it feels like I’m walking through a minefield, hoping I don’t trigger a memory I’m not ready to relive.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

I think what’s missing is a space where I’m allowed to be a person, not a patient or a professional. Everyone talks about "clinical support," but no one asks about the human cost of the commute or the loneliness of these long, quiet afternoons. My husband is working himself to the bone to cover our costs, and I know he’s hurting too, but we’re both so focused on the logistics—getting to the clinic, paying the bills—that we barely have room to just *be* together. 

From my providers, I’m desperate for them to put down the clipboard for a second. I don’t need them to explain the cell division again—I know it by heart. I need someone to look me in the eye and say, "I know you're terrified. I see that you've been here before, and it’s okay to not be okay today." 

I feel like I’m constantly performing for them. I have to be the "smart patient" so they’ll take me seriously, but that role leaves no room for me to admit that I’m drowning. There’s no acknowledgment of the trauma of my past loss. It’s all about the future baby and the current test results, and it leaves me feeling like a vessel rather than a mother who is just trying to survive the journey to the finish line.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected to find a community. I thought I’d find forums or resources that felt like a sanctuary—a place where other women who’d been through loss were speaking in honest, raw terms. Since I write about this stuff, I’ve seen all the glossy, polished apps that track your ovulation with cute little icons. I expected something that balanced the medical precision with a bit of empathy.

But honestly? What I found was either aggressively optimistic or terrifyingly clinical. The apps are all "Your baby is the size of a poppyseed!"—which feels like a cruel joke given my history—or they’re just cold, hard data trackers that turn my cycle into a series of red flags and "optimal" windows. It’s all very sterilized. 

What I really needed was a place that acknowledged the duality: yes, the science is fascinating, but it’s also terrifying when you’ve seen it fail. Most of what’s out there online feels like it’s written for someone having a "textbook" experience. There’s almost nothing for the woman who knows *too much* and is grieving while she’s hoping. It just made me feel even more isolated, like I was the only person sitting on the train feeling like a broken machine in a world that only cares if I'm "functioning" correctly.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap is in the narrative. My healthcare providers focus almost exclusively on "mitigation." Because of my history and my age, their language is framed entirely around risk management. They talk about what *could* go wrong, which tests to run to catch an anomaly, and how to keep my blood pressure in a specific range. It’s all about the mechanics of the machinery—my body as a system that needs constant monitoring to ensure a successful output.

Conversely, the digital sources and apps I see are mostly "performance art." They push this relentless, toxic positivity. They use emojis and upbeat language to tell me to "enjoy the glow" or track my symptoms like it’s a fun hobby. Neither side tells the truth of where I actually live: the middle ground. 

My doctor talks about the *probability* of loss, while the apps talk about the *joy* of the journey. Neither of them speaks to the person who is doing both: holding the terrifying data in one hand and the fragile, desperate hope in the other. No one tells you how to balance the clinical reality with the emotional wreckage. I’m left to bridge that chasm myself, and frankly, I don’t think I have the strength for it anymore.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I suppose I expected a roadmap. Since I have a history that puts me in this "medium risk" category, I thought the healthcare system would treat my preconception phase as a deliberate, thoughtful preparation period. I envisioned a sit-down where someone would review my medical history, acknowledge the grief of my previous loss, and say, "Deidre, here is how we are going to optimize your health *and* your mental well-being before we even try to conceive." I wanted a plan that felt human-centered.

In reality? It was just a series of "clearances." It felt less like preparation and more like a series of gates I had to pass through to prove I was viable. I went to specialists, but it was just more of the same—more blood draws, more waiting in that same uncomfortable chair, and more of me explaining *my* own history to people who hadn’t even bothered to look at my chart before walking through the door. 

Nobody asked me how I was managing the anxiety of trying again. Nobody asked if I had a support system to help with the physical exhaustion of getting to appointments. They cleared my body, but they completely ignored the person holding it together. It’s like they fixed the pipes but didn't notice the house was still burning.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

Advocate? I feel like I’m constantly shouting into a wind tunnel. The system doesn’t reach out—it waits for you to show up, and if you don't show up with the right questions or the right terminology, you just fall through the cracks. 

I’ve had to be my own case manager. I’m the one calling to ensure my records from the previous specialist were actually faxed over. I’m the one pushing for specific screenings because I’ve read the literature and I know what my risk profile *actually* looks like, rather than what the standard template suggests. It’s exhausting to have to prove you’re "informed enough" to be taken seriously, but that’s the only way I can get them to look past the surface. 

If I wasn’t a writer who knows how to parse medical journals, I don’t know how I’d manage. That’s the part that keeps me up at night—what about the women who don't have this background? If I have to fight this hard to be heard, what chance do they have? The system is designed for people who can advocate for themselves, but when you’re already grieving and nauseous and commuting across the city alone, you don't always have the energy to be your own warrior. I shouldn't have to be. I just need a doctor, not an adversary.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I had this romanticized image of us in this together—a team, unified in our intent. I pictured us sitting at the kitchen table, talking through our hopes, researching together, and him physically being there for the appointments, holding my hand while we navigated the scary parts. I wanted that shared weight; I thought if we both carried it, neither of us would be crushed.

But the reality is that the financial reality of our life in Boston has fractured that vision. My husband is working double shifts, constantly chasing the overtime he needs just so we can afford this—and the prospect of a child. He’s physically exhausted and mentally drained. When he *is* home, he’s often asleep or too wiped out to really dive into the emotional side of what I’m going through. 

It’s not that he doesn't care; it’s that he’s in survival mode, and I’m in survival mode, but we’re surviving in different rooms. I find myself shielding him from the worst of my fears because he’s already carrying the weight of our future, and I don't want to break him. We’re passing each other like ships in the night. I thought we’d be building a nest, but instead, it feels like we’re just scrambling to keep the walls from caving in. It’s the loneliest kind of partnership.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

Oh, every single moment. The burden of preparation feels less like a shared task and more like a second, full-time job that I’m working without a paycheck. 

It’s everything—the logistics, the research, the emotional labor. I’m the one navigating the MBTA’s broken schedules to get to the clinic. I’m the one managing the calendar of tests and the insurance paperwork that feels designed to confuse me. But it’s deeper than just chores. I’m the one who carries the "ghost" of our last pregnancy, the one who does the emotional heavy lifting so my husband doesn’t have to see me fall apart. I curate the information, I translate the medical jargon, and I curate my own anxiety so I don't bleed onto him.

What drives it? It’s the sheer vulnerability of being the one carrying the pregnancy. Because the biological reality is happening inside *my* body, the system treats me as the primary owner of the process. My doctors address me, the apps send the notifications to me, and society expects me to know exactly what’s happening in my own uterus. It feels like I’m the captain of a sinking ship, and everyone else is just watching to see if I can bail out enough water to keep us afloat. I’m tired of being the only one holding the bucket.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I think I expected—or maybe just desperately hoped for—a transition. I thought that after the loss, the system would have some sort of bridge or follow-up protocol. I expected them to say, "We know what you went through, and because of that, our approach to your next pregnancy will be different." I wanted a bit of extra grace, maybe an earlier appointment, or a provider who would just acknowledge the emotional shadow that follows me. I wanted to feel like my history mattered, like it had changed how they would care for me.

The reality was… jarring. When I walked back in, it was like hitting a "reset" button. I was just a new patient with a code in my chart. No one asked how I was processing the trauma of the last time; no one offered a counseling referral or even a mention of how anxiety might color this experience. It was as if that loss had been filed away in a cabinet, irrelevant to the current "medium risk" calculation. 

I’m left with this deep sense of invisibility. I’m carrying the memory of that silence in the ultrasound room, and I’m expected to show up to the next one like I haven't been scarred by the last. The support isn't there, so I’m just layering my new fear on top of my old grief, alone.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

Yes. Every time I walk into that office. There was one specific appointment recently—it was a routine check-up, and the technician was moving through the motions so quickly, clicking buttons and typing data. I asked a question about a specific marker, trying to connect it to what happened last time, and she just gave me this practiced, placid smile and said, "Don't worry, Mom, everything looks standard for now."

It felt like a slap. That word—*standard*—is the most dangerous word in the world when you’ve had a loss. To them, "standard" is a comfort, a sign that the machine is working. To me, it’s a trigger. It tells me they aren’t looking at *my* story; they’re looking at a population average. That loss isn't just a clinical data point to me; it was the end of a world. When they treat my current anxiety as an inconvenience or a "normal first-trimester worry," they are effectively telling me that my grief is irrelevant because the current statistics say I'm "low risk" enough for them to dismiss my fear. 

I’m not a "standard" patient. I’m a woman who has already had her heart broken by their standard protocols. If they can't validate that, they can't really care for me.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think, if I could sum it up, it’s that there is a profound silence where there should be human connection. We talk about "maternity care" as if it’s just a series of medical checks and balances, but for those of us who have lived through loss, it’s a terrifying act of faith. 

What I want people to understand is that the clinical efficiency you pride yourselves on? It’s not actually "care" for someone like me. True care would be acknowledging that I am not just a vessel for a future human. I’m a person whose entire world was once shattered in the very rooms you’re now ushering me into. 

When you rush the ultrasound, or when you give me a standard pamphlet instead of asking if I’m sleeping, or when you make me fight to be heard, you aren't just ignoring my anxiety—you’re deepening my isolation. I’m not asking for someone to change the outcome, because I know you can’t. I’m asking for someone to sit in the dark with me while I’m waiting for the light to turn on. I’m asking to be treated with a tenderness that recognizes I’m holding my breath, hoping—against everything I know—that this time, it’s going to be okay.
